verses.text: Boaz goes straight to the gate and raises the matter openly before the elders. He does not scheme in secret but acts promptly, lawfully, in the light. A man who means to do right has no need of shadows; he settles things honestly and without delay.

Boaz goes straight to the gate and raises the matter openly before the elders. He does not scheme in secret but acts promptly, lawfully, in the light. A man who means to do right has no need of shadows; he settles things honestly and without delay.

Boaz goes straight to the gate and raises the matter openly before the elders. He does not scheme in secret but acts promptly, lawfully, in the light. A man who means to do right has no need of shadows; he settles things honestly and without delay.

The nearer kinsman is glad to take the land — until he learns it comes with Ruth and the duty to raise an heir. 'I cannot, lest I mar mine own inheritance,' he says, and steps back. Self-interest always declines the costly work of redemption.

The nearer kinsman is glad to take the land — until he learns it comes with Ruth and the duty to raise an heir. 'I cannot, lest I mar mine own inheritance,' he says, and steps back. Self-interest always declines the costly work of redemption.

Boaz takes the whole cost on himself — he buys the land and takes Ruth to raise up the dead man's name. What another would not pay, the true kinsman gladly pays. So our Redeemer took on Himself the price we could never meet, and claimed us as His own.

The people bless the marriage, praying the LORD would build Ruth into the house of Israel like Rachel and Leah. A foreign widow is welcomed into the covenant family with joy. Grace makes the outsider an heir, and the whole community rejoices to see it.

The people bless the marriage, praying the LORD would build Ruth into the house of Israel like Rachel and Leah. A foreign widow is welcomed into the covenant family with joy. Grace makes the outsider an heir, and the whole community rejoices to see it.

The LORD gives Ruth a son, Obed, and Naomi — once 'Mara,' bitter and empty — cradles a grandchild called 'a restorer of life.' The empty house is full again. And this child is grandfather to David: God turns famine and grief into the cradle of kings.

The book ends with a genealogy: Pharez to Boaz to Obed to Jesse to David. A story of two grieving women in a small town proves to be a link in the line of the Messiah. Through Ruth the Moabitess, grace runs straight on toward great David's greater Son.
